title: China's Emerging Hereditary Elite and Wealth Inequality
source: The Guardian - The Economist (Web Edition) 14 March 2026-1
content:
Due to the government's one-child policy, most urban dwellers abided by it, meaning the assets of two parents will soon go to a single heir. Even as wage gaps have narrowed slightly, accumulated wealth is starting to matter more for individual prosperity. This represents an abrupt transition for China, shifting from a belief in hard work to reliance on inherited assets. Meanwhile, steep declines in property prices have hurt middle-class Chinese, while the uber-wealthy have emerged in better shape. Persistent inequality adds to economic imbalances, as the well-off tend to spend less of their income than the poor.

Despite President Xi Jinping’s talk of greater equality, official thinking remains woefully behind the curve on inheritance and wealth redistribution. The Communist Party is opposed to significant redistribution of wealth, fearing that handouts will make people lazy. However, ignoring accumulated wealth will ensure that deep inequality becomes permanently ingrained. Observers note that China has inadvertently become a partial tax haven, lacking an inheritance tax and a recurring property tax. If inequality keeps rising unchecked, it can significantly damage the country's long-term economic growth.

Taxing wealth requires assets to be reported, which has stalled the launch of a property tax because many corrupt officials own multiple homes. Forcing political elites to come clean would expose pervasive graft and trigger a pre-emptive wave of home sales. China’s leaders have consistently been slow to correct obvious mistakes, such as ending the one-child policy or deflating the property bubble. Now, they face a slow-moving but visible problem regarding the transfer of vast riches. The danger is that they may soon wake up to a permanent wealthy elite ruling over a disillusioned society.
